profile.name: Xian Valencia
profile.bio: I write code by day and chase PRs by dawn. This is where shipping software meets logging miles — race reports, engineering notes, and everything in between.

posts.title: Engineering a Sub-2 Half Marathon: My Training Plan as a Sprint Backlog
posts.excerpt: I treated my 12-week half marathon block like a product sprint — with velocity charts, retros, and a definition of done. Here's the full breakdown.
body: Twelve weeks ago I committed to breaking two hours in the half marathon. As an engineer, I couldn't resist structuring the whole thing like a sprint backlog: weekly mileage as story points, long runs as epics, and every tempo session with its own acceptance criteria.
Week 1–4 was base building — the 'infrastructure sprint'. Nothing glamorous, just easy aerobic miles and strides, the running equivalent of setting up CI/CD before you write features.
Weeks 5–9 introduced tempo work and intervals. I logged everything to Strava and pulled my splits into a spreadsheet every Sunday for a personal retro: what went well, what hurt, what to adjust.
Race day: 1:57:42. Definition of done — met. The biggest lesson? Consistency beats intensity, in training blocks and in codebases alike.

posts.title: Building a Personal Strava Dashboard with Next.js and the Strava API
posts.excerpt: A weekend project: pulling my activities from the Strava API into a Next.js dashboard with charts, heatmaps, and zero backend servers.
body: Every runner-engineer eventually builds a Strava dashboard. It's a rite of passage, like writing your own blog engine (also guilty).

The stack: Next.js with static generation, the Strava OAuth flow for tokens, and a build-time fetch that turns my last 200 activities into JSON. No servers, no database — the whole thing redeploys nightly via GitHub Actions.
The most interesting problem was rate limiting. Strava allows 100 requests per 15 minutes, so the fetcher batches endpoints and caches aggressively.
Full source is on my GitHub. Fork it, plug in your tokens, and you'll have your own dashboard in under an hour.

posts.title: VLOG: Marathon Training, Week 1 — 42km Ahead
posts.excerpt: First week of the full marathon build is in the books. I vlogged every run — the 5AM alarms, the humidity, and the questionable carb-loading choices.
body: Week one of marathon training, documented on camera. Watch the full vlog below.
Highlights: a 16km long run in 89% humidity, my first attempt at making my own energy gels (do not recommend), and a cameo from every stray cat on my route.
Subscribe on YouTube to follow the whole 18-week build.

posts.title: What Running Taught Me About Debugging Production Incidents
posts.excerpt: Bonking at kilometer 30 and watching a service melt down at 3AM feel surprisingly similar. Both taught me the same three lessons.
body: Lesson one: panic wastes energy. When you bonk mid-race, flailing makes it worse — you slow down, take stock, and refuel. Same with a production incident: the first move is never a frantic redeploy.
Lesson two: instrument everything. You can't fix what you can't measure. Heart rate zones are just observability for your body.
Lesson three: recovery is part of the work. Rest days make you faster; blameless postmortems make systems stronger. Skipping either just accrues debt.
The overlap isn't a coincidence. Both disciplines are about sustained performance under constraints — and respecting the system you're running on.
